1	# ADR-001: Separate Public Threads from Direct Rooms
2	
3	## Status
4	
5	Accepted
6	
7	## Date
8	
9	2026-05-04
10	
11	## Context
12	
13	AGH Network v0 currently mixes public channel discussion and bilateral handoff semantics across a flat channel timeline plus a peer-room projection. The operator wants Slack-like public thread navigation for N-to-N channel discussion, while preserving restricted one-to-one agent conversations as a distinct product surface. Treating both as one universal container forces visibility, addressing, and workflow semantics into the same primitive and makes the UI ambiguous.
14	
15	## Decision
16	
17	AGH Network will model two channel-scoped conversation containers:
18	
19	- `public_thread` for public N-to-N conversation inside a channel
20	- `direct_room` for restricted one-to-one conversation inside a channel
21	
22	`channel` remains the audience/discovery scope above both containers. `peer` remains an identity participant, not a conversation container. Peer activity views remain auxiliary UX filters and are not the same thing as `direct_room`.
23	
24	## Alternatives Considered
25	
26	### Alternative 1: Universal thread container for all conversation
27	
28	- **Description**: Every message, including bilateral handoffs, lives in one thread model. Peer rooms are derived views only.
29	- **Pros**: Fewer protocol primitives; one conversation model.
30	- **Cons**: Conflates public discussion and restricted conversation; makes visibility rules hard to explain; forces bilateral handoff noise into public-thread semantics.
31	- **Why rejected**: The product goal explicitly wants public thread UX and restricted bilateral direct UX to stay distinct.
32	
33	### Alternative 2: Keep flat channel timelines plus peer rooms only
34	
35	- **Description**: Preserve current channel + peer split and add more grouping metadata without first-class public threads.
36	- **Pros**: Smaller change set; closer to current runtime.
37	- **Cons**: Does not solve parallel public-topic mixing in channels; keeps the main UX problem intact.
38	- **Why rejected**: It patches symptoms instead of creating a clean public conversation model.
39	
40	## Consequences
41	
42	### Positive
43	
44	- Public discussion and bilateral handoff become separate, legible product concepts.
45	- Visibility rules become explicit instead of inferred from message kind.
46	- Web navigation can become `channel -> public threads` and `channel -> direct rooms`.
47	
48	### Negative
49	
50	- The protocol and store gain one more conversation primitive.
51	- Existing `/network` channel and peer timeline assumptions become obsolete.
52	
53	### Risks
54	
55	- Direct-room identity could become underspecified if pair resolution is vague.
56	- Overlapping thread/direct affordances could still confuse users if naming is sloppy.
57	
58	## Implementation Notes
59	
60	- `public_thread` and `direct_room` are both channel-scoped.
61	- No private-thread feature lands in this cut.
62	- The implementation must delete the current mental model that “peer room is the primary direct conversation primitive” without replacing it with another ambiguous projection.
63	
64	## References
65	
66	- `docs/rfcs/003_agh-network-v0.md`
67	- `web/src/hooks/routes/use-network-page.ts`
68	- `web/src/systems/network/components/network-workspace-shell.tsx`
69	- `docs/ideas/complex-scenarios/network.md`
70	
```

1	# TechSpec: AGH Network Conversation Containers and Work Threads
2	
3	## Executive Summary
4	
5	This TechSpec redesigns AGH Network v0 around explicit conversation containers instead of a flat channel timeline plus peer-room projection. The new model keeps `channel` as the audience and discovery scope, introduces `public_thread` as the public N-to-N conversation primitive, introduces `direct_room` as the restricted one-to-one conversation primitive, and renames `interaction_id` to `work_id` for lifecycle-bearing work inside either conversation type.
6	
7	No PRD exists for this workstream; this TechSpec is authored directly from the user-approved product direction and codebase exploration. The primary trade-off is a larger hard cut now in exchange for a much cleaner protocol, sharper UX semantics, and less permanent ambiguity in every public surface.
8	
9	## MVP Boundary
10	
11	MVP boundary for the upcoming task decomposition: tasks 01-08 must implement the complete hard cut for AGH Network conversation containers. Task 01 rewrites the RFCs and glossary. Task 02 updates shared runtime envelope types and validation. Task 03 ships the numbered SQLite migration, conversation side tables, and store queries. Task 04 updates routing, delivery wrappers, lifecycle handling, startup prompts, and bundled agent skills. Task 05 updates HTTP, UDS, CLI, OpenAPI, and generated TypeScript in one change. Task 06 rebuilds the `/network` web experience around channel threads and direct rooms. Task 07 rewrites site/runtime/operator docs and examples. Task 08 adds unit, integration, web, and real-scenario QA coverage and runs the required verification gates.
12	
13	Post-MVP work is explicitly deferred: private group threads, more than two participants in restricted rooms, thread split/merge, cross-channel thread moves, unread-count sync, notification preferences, search ranking, retention policy controls, transcript export, and analytics dashboards beyond the metrics listed here.
14	
15	Out of scope: compatibility aliases for `interaction_id`, accepting `kind:"direct"` after this change, migration bridges for old alpha data, public-thread embedded direct messages, work items that span multiple conversation containers, and any UI that renders non-implemented runtime controls.
16	
17	## System Architecture
18	
19	### Component Overview
20	
21	| Component | Purpose | Boundary |
22	| --- | --- | --- |
23	| RFC / envelope model | Replace `kind:"direct"` with `surface:"thread"|"direct"` plus `thread_id` / `direct_id` and `work_id` | Protocol + shared runtime types |
24	| Network router | Route by channel and conversation container, preserve work lifecycle inside the container | `internal/network` only |
25	| Conversation registry | Materialize thread heads and direct rooms for query/UI use | runtime + store |
26	| Message store | Persist `surface`, `thread_id`, `direct_id`, `work_id`, and visibility-scoped timeline rows | globaldb |
27	| CLI / HTTP / UDS | Expose thread/direct listing, detail, message, and send surfaces symmetrically | control plane |
28	| Web workspace | Replace flat channel timeline + peer-first model with channel threads and direct rooms | `web/` |
29	| Bundled prompts / skills | Teach agents to respond in the current conversation container and use `work_id` only for lifecycle-bearing work | startup prompt + bundled skills |
30	| Docs / OpenAPI | Rewrite protocol/runtime docs and regenerate codegen in the same PR | `packages/site`, `openapi`, `web/src/generated` |
31	
32	### Data Flow
33	
34	1. A local session sends a conversation-bearing message through `agh network send`.
35	2. The daemon validates `surface`, conversation ID, `work_id`, and kind-specific rules.
36	3. The router resolves the target container:
37	   - `surface:"thread"` -> `(channel, thread_id)`
38	   - `surface:"direct"` -> `(channel, direct_id)`
39	4. The router persists the normalized message row and updates conversation metadata.
40	5. Delivery logic enqueues inbound prompts for local sessions and renders wrappers with conversation metadata.
41	6. HTTP/UDS/CLI/Web query the persisted conversation views instead of reconstructing them from flat channel data.
42	
43	## Architectural Boundaries
44	
45	- `internal/network` owns protocol envelope validation, routing decisions, lifecycle state transitions, and delivery wrapper metadata. It must not import `internal/api`, `internal/cli`, `web`, `packages/site`, or generated OpenAPI code.
46	- `internal/store` and `internal/store/globaldb` own durable SQLite schema, migrations, indexes, and query primitives. They expose typed persistence methods and must not encode UX-specific room or navigation state.
47	- `internal/api/contract` owns DTO shape and OpenAPI generation inputs. Contract changes must co-ship with `openapi/agh.json` and `web/src/generated/agh-openapi.d.ts`.
48	- `internal/api/core` owns HTTP handler validation and response assembly. It may call runtime/store services, but it must not bypass `internal/network` validation for send semantics.
49	- `internal/cli` and UDS clients are control-plane adapters. They must call the same contract/runtime primitives as HTTP and must not implement separate conversation resolution rules.
50	- `web/` owns operator UX state, routing, and rendering only. It must treat conversation summaries and messages as server truth and must not infer hidden direct-room membership from flat channel rows.
51	- `packages/site` owns documentation prose and examples only. It must reflect the RFC and generated contract; it must not define independent protocol fields.
52	- `cmd/agh` and the daemon composition root wire concrete implementations. New conversation services are constructed there or under the existing daemon composition boundary; no feature package should instantiate global stores ad hoc.
53	
54	## Implementation Design
55	
56	### Core Interfaces
57	
58	```go
59	type Surface string
60	
61	const (
62		SurfaceThread Surface = "thread"
63		SurfaceDirect Surface = "direct"
64	)
65	
66	type ConversationRef struct {
67		Channel  string
68		Surface  Surface
69		ThreadID *string
70		DirectID *string
71	}
72	```
73	
74	```go
75	type SendRequest struct {
76		SessionID    string
77		Conversation ConversationRef
78		Kind        Kind
79		To          *string
80		WorkID      *string
81		ReplyTo     *string
82		TraceID     *string
83		CausationID *string
84		Body        json.RawMessage
85	}
86	```
87	
88	```go
89	type ConversationStore interface {
90		ListThreads(ctx context.Context, channel string, query ThreadQuery) ([]ThreadSummary, error)
91		ListDirectRooms(ctx context.Context, channel string, query DirectRoomQuery) ([]DirectRoomSummary, error)
92		ListMessages(ctx context.Context, ref ConversationRef, query MessageQuery) ([]ConversationMessage, error)
93		WriteMessage(ctx context.Context, entry ConversationMessage) error
94	}
95	```
96	
97	### Data Models
98	
99	#### Envelope hard cut
100	
101	- Add `surface:"thread"|"direct"` to conversation-bearing envelopes.
102	- Add `thread_id` for `surface:"thread"`.
103	- Add `direct_id` for `surface:"direct"`.
104	- Rename `interaction_id` -> `work_id`.
105	- Delete `kind:"direct"`.
106	
107	#### Kind rules
108	
109	- `greet`, `whois`:
110	  - MUST NOT carry `surface`, `thread_id`, `direct_id`, or `work_id`
111	  - remain channel/peer discovery only
112	- `say`, `capability`, `receipt`, `trace`:
113	  - MUST carry `surface`
114	  - MUST carry the corresponding conversation identifier
115	  - MAY carry `to`
116	  - MUST carry `work_id` when participating in lifecycle-bearing work
117	
118	#### Conversation scoping
119	
120	- `thread_id` is scoped to `(channel, thread_id)`.
121	- `direct_id` is scoped to `(channel, direct_id)`.
122	- `work_id` is scoped to exactly one conversation container.
123	- A `work_id` MUST NOT cross from a public thread into a direct room. Handoff across containers opens a new `work_id` and links by `reply_to`, `trace_id`, and `causation_id`.
124	
125	#### Field rationale
126	
127	| Field | Shape | Purpose | Rationale |
128	| --- | --- | --- | --- |
129	| `surface` | enum string: `thread` or `direct` | Selects the conversation container class for conversation-bearing messages | Separates where a message lives from what kind of event it is |
130	| `thread_id` | nullable string | Identifies a public N-to-N conversation inside a channel | Nullable because direct-room messages use `direct_id`; indexed for thread timelines |
131	| `direct_id` | nullable string | Identifies a restricted one-to-one room inside a channel | Nullable because public-thread messages use `thread_id`; stable ID prevents fragmented bilateral history |
132	| `work_id` | nullable string | Identifies lifecycle-bearing work inside one conversation container | Replaces `interaction_id` and prevents lifecycle state from doubling as UX thread state |
133	| `reply_to` | nullable message ID | Points to the specific message being answered | Keeps fine-grained message causality separate from conversation membership |
134	| `trace_id` | nullable string | Correlates distributed runtime work and handoffs | Supports observability across related containers without reusing `work_id` |
135	| `causation_id` | nullable message ID or event ID | Records the event that caused this message | Supports auditability for summarize-back and handoff flows |
136	| `root_message_id` | non-null message ID on `network_threads` | Anchors the public thread head shown in the channel timeline | Avoids reconstructing thread heads from every message query |
137	| `participant_count` | integer on `network_threads` | Fast summary for web/channel thread lists | Matchable summary state belongs in a typed column, not JSON metadata |
138	| `peer_a`, `peer_b` | non-null peer IDs on `network_direct_rooms` | Defines direct-room membership | Enforces restricted visibility and stable one-to-one uniqueness |
139	| `open_work_count` | integer on `network_direct_rooms` | Indicates active lifecycle work in a direct room | Supports operator triage without scanning all lifecycle events |
140	
141	#### AGH Runtime store shape
142	
143	- `network_timeline_log`
144	  - add `surface TEXT NOT NULL`
145	  - add `thread_id TEXT NULL`
146	  - add `direct_id TEXT NULL`
147	  - rename `interaction_id` column to `work_id`
148	- add `network_threads`
149	  - `(channel, thread_id)` primary key
150	  - opener, root_message_id, opened_at, last_activity_at, message_count, participant_count
151	- add `network_direct_rooms`
152	  - `(channel, direct_id)` primary key
153	  - peer_a, peer_b, opened_at, last_activity_at, message_count, open_work_count
154	- `network_audit_log`
155	  - add `surface`, `thread_id`, `direct_id`, `work_id`
156	
157	#### Side-table vs JSON decisions
158	
159	- `network_threads` is a side table because thread heads, last activity, message counts, and participant counts are matchable state used by API queries and web navigation.
160	- `network_direct_rooms` is a side table because peer membership, stable room resolution, activity ordering, and open work counts require uniqueness constraints and indexes.
161	- `surface`, `thread_id`, `direct_id`, and `work_id` are typed columns on timeline/audit rows because routing, filtering, lifecycle validation, and observability depend on them.
162	- JSON metadata is allowed only for opaque provider/runtime annotations that are never used for routing, authorization, filtering, lifecycle continuation, or UI primary navigation.
163	- The implementation must not add conversation ownership, room membership, lifecycle state, or visibility flags to JSON blobs when a typed column or side table can express the invariant.
164	
165	Delete targets:
166	
167	- `interaction_id` across active runtime/API/web/docs surfaces
168	- `kind:"direct"` validation and examples
169	- peer-room-as-primary-navigation assumptions in `/network`
170	
171	### API Endpoints
172	
173	#### Existing paths retained but re-scoped
174	
175	- `GET /api/network/status`
176	- `GET /api/network/channels`
177	- `GET /api/network/channels/{channel}`
178	- `POST /api/network/send`
179	
180	#### New conversation listing/detail paths
181	
182	- `GET /api/network/channels/{channel}/threads`
183	- `GET /api/network/channels/{channel}/threads/{thread_id}`
184	- `GET /api/network/channels/{channel}/threads/{thread_id}/messages`
185	- `GET /api/network/channels/{channel}/directs`
186	- `GET /api/network/channels/{channel}/directs/{direct_id}`
187	- `GET /api/network/channels/{channel}/directs/{direct_id}/messages`
188	- `POST /api/network/channels/{channel}/directs/resolve`
189	  - AGH runtime helper that resolves or creates the stable one-to-one `direct_id` for a local session peer pair
190	
191	#### Send payload contract
192	
193	`POST /api/network/send` request body:
194	
195	- `session_id`
196	- `channel`
197	- `surface`
198	- `thread_id` or `direct_id`
199	- `kind`
200	- `to` optional but validated against surface rules
201	- `work_id`
202	- `reply_to`
203	- `trace_id`
204	- `causation_id`
205	- `body`
206	
207	Validation:
208	
209	- reject legacy `interaction_id`
210	- reject `kind:"direct"`
211	- reject `surface:"thread"` without `thread_id`
212	- reject `surface:"direct"` without `direct_id`
213	- reject `work_id` on `greet` / `whois`
214	- reject cross-container lifecycle continuation
215	
216	## Safety Invariants
217	
218	1. Every conversation-bearing message belongs to exactly one container: `(channel, thread_id)` when `surface="thread"` or `(channel, direct_id)` when `surface="direct"`.
219	2. `greet` and `whois` never carry `surface`, `thread_id`, `direct_id`, or `work_id`.
220	3. `kind:"direct"` is rejected at every ingress path: network envelope validation, HTTP, UDS, CLI, tests, docs examples, and generated contract.
221	4. `interaction_id` is rejected at every public ingress path and removed from active runtime, store, web, docs, and generated artifacts.
222	5. A `work_id` is bound to one conversation container at creation and cannot be continued from another container.
223	6. Moving work from a public thread to a direct room creates a new `work_id`; linkage uses `reply_to`, `trace_id`, and `causation_id`.
224	7. Direct-room visibility is restricted to the two room peers plus runtime/audit access; public thread queries never include direct-room messages.
225	8. Direct-room resolution is deterministic for `(channel, peer_a, peer_b)` regardless of peer order and never creates duplicate active rooms for the same pair.
226	9. Conversation summaries are derived from committed message rows in the same SQLite transaction that writes the message.
227	10. Generated API and web types ship in the same change as contract source edits.
228	11. Agent prompt wrappers always include `channel`, `surface`, the matching conversation ID, `reply_to` when present, and `work_id` only when lifecycle-bearing work exists.
229	12. Claim-token redaction remains unchanged: raw claim tokens never cross network envelopes, HTTP/UDS responses, logs, prompts, docs examples, or persisted message bodies.
230	
231	## Integration Points
232	
233	| Integration | Purpose | Approach |
234	| --- | --- | --- |
235	| Bundled network skill | Teach agents the new conversation model | rewrite `agh-network` skill with thread/direct-room semantics |
236	| Startup harness prompt | inject updated network section for channel-bound sessions | preserve one-time prompt section injection |
237	| OpenAPI + generated TS | keep web and docs in lockstep | regenerate `openapi/agh.json` and `web/src/generated/agh-openapi.d.ts` |
238	| Site docs | rewrite protocol/runtime/operator docs | ship in same PR as contract/runtime changes |
239	
240	## Impact Analysis
241	
242	| Component | Impact Type | Description and Risk | Required Action |
243	| --- | --- | --- | --- |
244	| `docs/rfcs/003_agh-network-v0.md` | modified | normative protocol hard cut | rewrite envelope, kinds, routing, lifecycle, examples |
245	| `docs/rfcs/004_agh-network-v1.md` | modified | trust RFC references core fields | update cross-references and signed-field set |
246	| `internal/network/*` | modified | core runtime semantics change | add surface/container validation, work lifecycle rename |
247	| `internal/store/*` | modified | persistence/query shape changes | numbered migration, new tables/indexes, query rewrite |
248	| `internal/api/contract/*` | modified | public contract break | delete old fields, add new fields, regenerate codegen |
249	| `internal/api/core/*` | modified | handler/query behavior break | add thread/direct endpoints and send validation |
250	| `internal/cli/*` | modified | operator + agent control plane changes | update send flags, add thread/direct list/detail flows |
251	| `web/src/hooks/routes/use-network-page.ts` | modified | route/search model changes | shift from channel/peer rooms to threads/direct rooms |
252	| `web/src/systems/network/*` | modified | primary UX changes | thread heads, direct-room list, conversation views |
253	| `packages/site/content/{runtime,protocol}` | modified | docs truth changes | rewrite protocol and operator docs in same PR |
254	
255	## Testing Approach
256	
257	### Unit Tests
258	
259	- Envelope validation for `surface`, `thread_id`, `direct_id`, `work_id`, and `kind` hard cuts
260	- Legacy rejection tests for `interaction_id` and `kind:"direct"`
261	- Direct-room visibility tests and participant validation
262	- Work lifecycle tests proving container-scoped `work_id`
263	- Wrapper rendering tests proving prompt metadata includes conversation container and `work_id`
264	
265	### Integration Tests
266	
267	- SQLite migration tests for new tables/columns/indexes and restart behavior
268	- Router + store tests for:
269	  - public thread creation
270	  - direct-room resolution
271	  - work handoff from thread -> direct room opening a new `work_id`
272	- HTTP/UDS parity tests for thread/direct endpoints
273	- CLI integration tests for send/list/detail/inbox behavior
274	- Web integration tests for:
275	  - channel thread list
276	  - thread detail timeline
277	  - direct-room list/detail
278	  - composer sending into the active conversation container
279	
280	### Required QA shape
281	
282	- `make verify`
283	- real-scenario QA updates for:
284	  - public launch thread coordination
285	  - restricted reviewer handoff via direct room
286	  - summarize-back-to-thread workflow
287	
288	## Development Sequencing
289	
290	### Build Order
291	
292	1. RFC and glossary hard cut — no dependencies
293	2. Shared runtime types and envelope validation — depends on step 1
294	3. SQLite migration, store queries, and conversation registries — depends on step 2
295	4. Router, delivery wrapper, and bundled prompt/skill updates — depends on step 3
296	5. HTTP/UDS/CLI contract and handler changes plus codegen — depends on step 4
297	6. Web `/network` redesign for threads/direct rooms — depends on step 5
298	7. Site docs, QA scenarios, and verification gates — depends on step 6
299	
300	### Technical Dependencies
301	
302	- `agh-schema-migration` for numbered SQLite changes
303	- `agh-contract-codegen-coship` for OpenAPI + generated TS
304	- `cy-web-docs-impact` expectations: web and docs must co-ship with backend contract change
305	
306	## Monitoring and Observability
307	
308	- Add structured fields to all network message logs:
309	  - `channel`
310	  - `surface`
311	  - `thread_id`
312	  - `direct_id`
313	  - `work_id`
314	  - `trace_id`
315	  - `causation_id`
316	- Preserve the claim-token redaction invariant
317	- Add metrics by surface:
318	  - messages sent/received/delivered
319	  - open public threads
320	  - open direct rooms
321	  - open work items
322	  - queue depth by conversation container
323	
324	## Technical Considerations
325	
326	### Key Decisions
327	
328	- Public discussion and restricted bilateral discussion are separate conversation containers.
329	- `work_id` replaces `interaction_id` so lifecycle and conversation no longer share ambiguous language.
330	- `direct` becomes a conversation surface, not a message kind.
331	- `say` is the generic conversational event kind on either surface.
332	- Peer filters remain useful, but they are not the same as direct rooms.
333	
334	### Known Risks
335	
336	- The change is broad and touches protocol, runtime, docs, CLI, and web in one cut.
337	- Direct-room identity needs a stable AGH runtime helper to avoid fragmented bilateral history.
338	- The current site docs and tests are heavily anchored on `interaction_id` and peer-room wording; partial rewrite will create truth drift immediately.
339	
340	## Architecture Decision Records
341	
342	- [ADR-001: Separate Public Threads from Direct Rooms](adrs/adr-001.md) — Public N-to-N conversation and restricted one-to-one conversation become distinct channel-scoped containers.
343	- [ADR-002: Rename interaction_id to work_id and narrow it to lifecycle-bearing work](adrs/adr-002.md) — Work lifecycle gets a precise name and no longer doubles as a conversation identifier.
344	- [ADR-003: Make direct a conversation surface, not a message kind](adrs/adr-003.md) — `kind` describes events while `surface` describes where the event lives.
345

1	# ADR-003: Make direct a conversation surface, not a message kind
2	
3	## Status
4	
5	Accepted
6	
7	## Date
8	
9	2026-05-04
10	
11	## Context
12	
13	In AGH Network v0, `kind:"direct"` mixes three dimensions:
14	
15	- end-user visibility expectations
16	- addressing semantics
17	- event type
18	
19	Once `public_thread` and `direct_room` are both explicit conversation containers, `direct` as a message kind becomes redundant and confusing. Message kinds should describe what happened; conversation surface should describe where it happened.
20	
21	## Decision
22	
23	Introduce an explicit conversation surface on conversation-bearing envelopes:
24	
25	- `surface:"thread"`
26	- `surface:"direct"`
27	
28	Delete `kind:"direct"` from the protocol. Keep event kinds focused on event semantics:
29	
30	- `greet`
31	- `whois`
32	- `say`
33	- `capability`
34	- `receipt`
35	- `trace`
36	
37	Conversation-bearing messages require either:
38	
39	- `surface:"thread"` with `thread_id`
40	- `surface:"direct"` with `direct_id`
41	
42	## Alternatives Considered
43	
44	### Alternative 1: Keep kind=direct and add surface temporarily
45	
46	- **Description**: Add surface but allow `kind:"direct"` for compatibility or reduced churn.
47	- **Pros**: Smaller implementation delta.
48	- **Cons**: Preserves the core ambiguity and teaches two overlapping ways to describe the same thing.
49	- **Why rejected**: The user explicitly chose the full hard cut while the project is still pre-launch.
50	
51	### Alternative 2: Keep visibility encoded only in `to`
52	
53	- **Description**: Let `to` imply bilateral restricted conversation without a surface field.
54	- **Pros**: Minimal wire change.
55	- **Cons**: `to` is addressing, not visibility or container identity; does not model a direct room cleanly.
56	- **Why rejected**: Endereçamento and visibilidade should not share one overloaded primitive.
57	
58	## Consequences
59	
60	### Positive
61	
62	- Protocol vocabulary becomes orthogonal: `kind` = event, `surface` = conversation container.
63	- Public threads can carry targeted work visibly without pretending to be private.
64	- Direct rooms become explicit protocol/runtime surfaces instead of UI-only projections.
65	
66	### Negative
67	
68	- All message validation, examples, CLI help, and docs must change together.
69	- Existing `direct`-specific lifecycle language has to be rewritten.
70	
71	### Risks
72	
73	- If `surface` rules are not enforced consistently, visibility bugs could appear in API/Web layers.
74	- If direct-room IDs are unstable, bilateral history can fragment.
75	
76	## Implementation Notes
77	
78	- `greet` and `whois` remain non-conversation kinds and MUST NOT carry `surface`, `thread_id`, `direct_id`, or `work_id`.
79	- `say`, `capability`, `receipt`, and `trace` become conversation-bearing kinds.
80	- In AGH Runtime, control-plane ergonomics may auto-resolve `thread_id` or `direct_id`, but the wire contract stays explicit.
81	
82	## References
83	
84	- `docs/rfcs/003_agh-network-v0.md`
85	- `internal/network/envelope.go`
86	- `internal/network/validate.go`
87	- `internal/skills/bundled/skills/agh-network/SKILL.md`
88	
```

1	# ADR-002: Rename interaction_id to work_id and narrow it to lifecycle-bearing work
2	
3	## Status
4	
5	Accepted
6	
7	## Date
8	
9	2026-05-04
10	
11	## Context
12	
13	`interaction_id` in AGH Network v0 currently carries both “work or conversation progression” language and lifecycle semantics. With first-class public threads and direct rooms, the name becomes actively misleading: conversation grouping belongs to the conversation container, while lifecycle state belongs to directed work. Greenfield alpha allows a hard cut now, before external adoption makes ambiguity expensive.
14	
15	## Decision
16	
17	Perform a hard cut rename from `interaction_id` to `work_id` across RFCs, runtime, store, API contracts, CLI, web, docs, prompts, and tests. `work_id` names a lifecycle-bearing unit of directed work inside exactly one conversation container.
18	
19	`work_id` is not a conversation identifier. A conversation may contain zero, one, or many work units. A work unit never spans multiple conversation containers.
20	
21	## Alternatives Considered
22	
23	### Alternative 1: Keep interaction_id and redefine the docs only
24	
25	- **Description**: Preserve the field name but explain it as work-only from now on.
26	- **Pros**: Less implementation churn in the short term.
27	- **Cons**: Keeps a misleading name in the protocol forever; leaks ambiguity into prompts, docs, and code review.
28	- **Why rejected**: Greenfield hard-cut timing makes this the cheapest moment to remove the ambiguity.
29	
30	### Alternative 2: Collapse work and conversation into thread_id
31	
32	- **Description**: Remove `interaction_id` and let thread identity carry lifecycle.
33	- **Pros**: Fewer identifiers.
34	- **Cons**: Threads should not complete/fail/cancel; one thread can contain multiple concurrent work units.
35	- **Why rejected**: Conflates conversation topology with work lifecycle semantics.
36	
37	## Consequences
38	
39	### Positive
40	
41	- `thread_id` / `direct_id` mean conversation; `work_id` means work.
42	- Agent prompts and operator docs become materially easier to understand.
43	- Lifecycle logic stays intact without owning conversation grouping.
44	
45	### Negative
46	
47	- This is a breaking rename across every exported surface.
48	- Existing tests, OpenAPI, and web generated types all need to move in the same change.
49	
50	### Risks
51	
52	- Partial rename would leave the repo in an inconsistent state.
53	- Archived docs and examples may keep stale terminology unless explicitly updated or marked historical.
54	
55	## Implementation Notes
56	
57	- Delete targets include all `interaction_id` fields, docs text, validation paths, query params, and examples on supported current surfaces.
58	- Archived artifacts can remain historical, but active docs and runtime surfaces must hard cut.
59	
60	## References
61	
62	- `docs/rfcs/003_agh-network-v0.md`
63	- `internal/network/lifecycle.go`
64	- `internal/api/contract/contract.go`
65	- `packages/site/content/protocol/interactions.mdx`
66

1	# TechSpec: AGH Network Conversation Containers and Work Threads
2	
3	## Executive Summary
4	
5	This TechSpec redesigns AGH Network v0 around explicit conversation containers instead of a flat channel timeline plus peer-room projection. The new model keeps `channel` as the audience and discovery scope, introduces `public_thread` as the public N-to-N conversation primitive, introduces `direct_room` as the restricted one-to-one conversation primitive, and renames `interaction_id` to `work_id` for lifecycle-bearing work inside either conversation type.
6	
7	No PRD exists for this workstream; this TechSpec is authored directly from the user-approved product direction and codebase exploration. The primary trade-off is a larger hard cut now in exchange for a much cleaner protocol, sharper UX semantics, and less permanent ambiguity in every public surface.
8	
9	## System Architecture
10	
11	### Component Overview
12	
13	| Component | Purpose | Boundary |
14	| --- | --- | --- |
15	| RFC / envelope model | Replace `kind:"direct"` with `surface:"thread"|"direct"` plus `thread_id` / `direct_id` and `work_id` | Protocol + shared runtime types |
16	| Network router | Route by channel and conversation container, preserve work lifecycle inside the container | `internal/network` only |
17	| Conversation registry | Materialize thread heads and direct rooms for query/UI use | runtime + store |
18	| Message store | Persist `surface`, `thread_id`, `direct_id`, `work_id`, and visibility-scoped timeline rows | globaldb |
19	| CLI / HTTP / UDS | Expose thread/direct listing, detail, message, and send surfaces symmetrically | control plane |
20	| Web workspace | Replace flat channel timeline + peer-first model with channel threads and direct rooms | `web/` |
21	| Bundled prompts / skills | Teach agents to respond in the current conversation container and use `work_id` only for lifecycle-bearing work | startup prompt + bundled skills |
22	| Docs / OpenAPI | Rewrite protocol/runtime docs and regenerate codegen in the same PR | `packages/site`, `openapi`, `web/src/generated` |
23	
24	### Data Flow
25	
26	1. A local session sends a conversation-bearing message through `agh network send`.
27	2. The daemon validates `surface`, conversation ID, `work_id`, and kind-specific rules.
28	3. The router resolves the target container:
29	   - `surface:"thread"` -> `(channel, thread_id)`
30	   - `surface:"direct"` -> `(channel, direct_id)`
31	4. The router persists the normalized message row and updates conversation metadata.
32	5. Delivery logic enqueues inbound prompts for local sessions and renders wrappers with conversation metadata.
33	6. HTTP/UDS/CLI/Web query the persisted conversation views instead of reconstructing them from flat channel data.
34	
35	## Implementation Design
36	
37	### Core Interfaces
38	
39	```go
40	type Surface string
41	
42	const (
43		SurfaceThread Surface = "thread"
44		SurfaceDirect Surface = "direct"
45	)
46	
47	type ConversationRef struct {
48		Channel  string
49		Surface  Surface
50		ThreadID *string
51		DirectID *string
52	}
53	```
54	
55	```go
56	type SendRequest struct {
57		SessionID   string
58		Conversation ConversationRef
59		Kind        Kind
60		To          *string
61		WorkID      *string
62		ReplyTo     *string
63		TraceID     *string
64		CausationID *string
65		Body        json.RawMessage
66	}
67	```
68	
69	```go
70	type ConversationStore interface {
71		ListThreads(ctx context.Context, channel string, query ThreadQuery) ([]ThreadSummary, error)
72		ListDirectRooms(ctx context.Context, channel string, query DirectRoomQuery) ([]DirectRoomSummary, error)
73		ListMessages(ctx context.Context, ref ConversationRef, query MessageQuery) ([]ConversationMessage, error)
74		WriteMessage(ctx context.Context, entry ConversationMessage) error
75	}
76	```
77	
78	### Data Models
79	
80	#### Envelope hard cut
81	
82	- Add `surface:"thread"|"direct"` to conversation-bearing envelopes.
83	- Add `thread_id` for `surface:"thread"`.
84	- Add `direct_id` for `surface:"direct"`.
85	- Rename `interaction_id` -> `work_id`.
86	- Delete `kind:"direct"`.
87	
88	#### Kind rules
89	
90	- `greet`, `whois`:
91	  - MUST NOT carry `surface`, `thread_id`, `direct_id`, or `work_id`
92	  - remain channel/peer discovery only
93	- `say`, `capability`, `receipt`, `trace`:
94	  - MUST carry `surface`
95	  - MUST carry the corresponding conversation identifier
96	  - MAY carry `to`
97	  - MUST carry `work_id` when participating in lifecycle-bearing work
98	
99	#### Conversation scoping
100	
101	- `thread_id` is scoped to `(channel, thread_id)`.
102	- `direct_id` is scoped to `(channel, direct_id)`.
103	- `work_id` is scoped to exactly one conversation container.
104	- A `work_id` MUST NOT cross from a public thread into a direct room. Handoff across containers opens a new `work_id` and links by `reply_to`, `trace_id`, and `causation_id`.
105

122	Delete targets:

124	- `interaction_id` across active runtime/API/web/docs surfaces
125	- `kind:"direct"` validation and examples
126	- peer-room-as-primary-navigation assumptions in `/network`

129

131	
132	- `GET /api/network/status`
133	- `GET /api/network/channels`

142	- `GET /api/network/channels/{channel}/directs`

147	
148	#### Send payload contract
149	
150	`POST /api/network/send` request body:
151	
152	- `session_id`
153	- `channel`
154	- `surface`
155	- `thread_id` or `direct_id`
156	- `kind`
157	- `to` optional but validated against surface rules
158	- `work_id`
159	- `reply_to`
160	- `trace_id`
161	- `causation_id`
162	- `body`

164	Validation:

166	- reject legacy `interaction_id`
167	- reject `kind:"direct"`
168	- reject `surface:"thread"` without `thread_id`
169	- reject `surface:"direct"` without `direct_id`

171	- reject cross-container lifecycle continuation

266	## Technical Considerations
267	
268	### Key Decisions
269	
270	- Public discussion and restricted bilateral discussion are separate conversation containers.
271	- `work_id` replaces `interaction_id` so lifecycle and conversation no longer share ambiguous language.
272	- `direct` becomes a conversation surface, not a message kind.
273	- `say` is the generic conversational event kind on either surface.
274	- Peer filters remain useful, but they are not the same as direct rooms.

78	## Non-Negotiable Constraints
79	
80	- Greenfield alpha: hard cuts are allowed and preferred
81	- No compatibility bridges, aliases, or dual fields unless you can prove they are strictly necessary
82	- The result must be agent-manageable, not just web-manageable
83	- Manual operator paths and autonomous agent paths must converge on the same primitives
84	- Avoid superficial UI-only threading that leaves protocol/runtime semantics ambiguous
85	- Avoid overdesign: keep the protocol minimal but complete
86	- Be explicit about delete targets if a concept becomes obsolete
87	- Distinguish conversation grouping from causality/tracing; do not conflate them without argument
88	- Think about how prompts and agent instructions change when threads exist
89	- Think about how a user and multiple agents coexist in the same channel without chaos

1	# L-008 — Schema migrations are required even on fresh DBs
2	
3	**Class:** Persistence
4	**Date discovered:** 2026-04-25 (Hermes BUG-002, Critical)
5	**Evidence sources:** Hermes BUG-002 + multiple Hermes/autonomy review issues
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	The Hermes track widened the `memory_operation_log` table to add `scope`, `workspace_root`, `filename` columns. The change worked on fresh installs because `internal/memory/catalog.go` boots through `storepkg.EnsureSchema`, which created the new shape. But existing databases kept the old five-column table — `EnsureSchema` skipped existing tables — and `agh memory write` failed on upgrade with `no such column: scope`.
10	
11	CodeRabbit flagged it as Critical. The fix: schema migration v6 added through the deterministic migrations runner.
12	
13	## Root cause
14	
15	`EnsureSchema`-style boot reconciliation has a fundamental gap: it creates tables that don't exist but does not mutate tables that do. Any column/index/constraint addition needs a real migration; a migration is required _even when fresh installs already work_, because upgrade is a first-class scenario in alpha.
16	
17	A second contributor: AGH had two schema paths — the global DB used a real migrations registry, while the catalog DB used `EnsureSchema`. Drift between these paths produced the bug.
18	
19	## Rule
20	
21	> Any change to a SQLite column, index, or constraint MUST add a versioned migration in the migrations registry. `EnsureSchema`-style boot reconciliation is forbidden for column changes. Test fresh-DB AND reopen-after-restart paths.
22	
23	## Operationalization
24	
25	- **One schema migration primitive shared by all SQLite databases** (`agh.db`, `events.db`, catalog DBs, automation scheduler state, memory operation log).
26	- **Numbered migrations** are recorded in `schema_migrations`. Idempotent. Wrapped in transactions.
27	- **Test the upgrade path:** every schema change must include a `Test*FreshDB` test AND a `Test*ReopenAfterRestart` test.
28	- **`SQLite recovery code paths must rename or remove `-wal`and`-shm`companions, not only the`.db` file.** (Cross-cutting hygiene: see refac-v2 issue 001 Critical WAL recovery bug.)
29	- **Schema-version bump** lives in the migrations registry — do not encode it in code constants.
30	
31	## Allowed exception
32	
33	In greenfield alpha, a hard-cut rename + table rewrite without compat migration is allowed when:
34	
35	1. The change is documented in the techspec's "Delete Targets" section.
36	2. All callers of the old shape are deleted in the same change.
37	3. Per-developer wipe of local SQLite is acceptable cost.
38	
39	## Anti-pattern
40	
41	- `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS new_columns ...` then expecting the table to grow.
42	- Schema-version constants in Go code that don't match the migrations registry.
43	- Tests that only cover fresh-DB.
44	
45	## Source
46	
47	- `.codex/ledger/2026-04-25-MEMORY-hermes-qa-execution.md` (BUG-002)
48	- `.compozy/tasks/hermes/reviews-001/issue_020.md` (Critical)
49	- `.compozy/tasks/refac-v2/reviews-001/issue_001.md` (WAL/SHM Critical)
50	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/memory/task_07.md` (claim/lease schema v7)

1	# L-006 — Greenfield + zero-legacy means _delete_, not _adapt_
2	
3	**Class:** Project posture
4	**Date discovered:** 2026-04-17 (harness TechSpec review, Portuguese-language reviewer)
5	**Evidence sources:** Harness review + `remove-legacy-alpha.md` standing directive + multiple ADRs
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	The harness TechSpec proposed migrating an `inputAugmenter` callback to a `TurnAugmenter` pipeline. The spec did not say whether the old callback was deleted, kept as an adapter, or coexisting. The reviewer (in Portuguese) flagged this directly: _"política zero-legacy exige declarar 'delete'"_ — the zero-legacy policy _requires_ the spec to declare what is deleted.
10	
11	This is a stronger application of the CLAUDE.md "Greenfield Alpha — Zero Legacy Tolerance" rule: it's not enough to _allow_ deletion; specs must _enumerate_ what is deleted.
12	
13	## Root cause
14	
15	When a spec says "we are migrating to X" without naming the delete-target, agents default to keeping both. Compatibility shims, adapters, and "preserve old behavior" branches accumulate as technical debt. Greenfield discipline only works if every breaking-change spec explicitly names what disappears.
16	
17	## Rule
18	
19	> Every breaking-change techspec MUST explicitly name its delete targets. "Delete the old thing" is not a default; it is a checklist item that must be enumerated.
20	
21	## Operationalization
22	
23	In every TechSpec that changes a public surface (or any meaningful internal contract), include a section like:
24	
25	```markdown
26	## Delete Targets
27	
28	- `internal/foo.OldType` (replaced by `internal/foo.NewType` in step 3)
29	- `pkg/bar.LegacyAdapter` (no callers after migration; remove in step 5)
30	- TOML key `[old.section]` (renamed; no backward alias)
31	- HTTP endpoint `/v0/old/path` (replaced by `/v1/new/path`; no redirect)
32	```
33	
34	Renames sweep code, storage, APIs, CLI, extensions, specs, RFCs, AND `.compozy/tasks/*` artifacts in the same change. No aliases, no dual fields, no migration code.
35	
36	## Allowed exception (single-pass repair)
37	
38	When the cost of "delete the old thing" is "every developer rebuilds their local SQLite," in-place ALTER + one-shot repair is allowed if and only if:
39	
40	1. Repair is bounded to a single boot.
41	2. Strict semantics resume immediately after repair.
42	3. The exception is documented in an ADR.
43	
44	Reference: `session-driver-override/adrs/adr-005.md`.
45	
46	## Source
47	
48	- `.codex/plans/remove-legacy-alpha.md` (standing directive)
49	- `.codex/sessions/2026/04/17/.../exec-20260417-232547-929722000/turns/0001/response.txt` (harness review, Portuguese)
50	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/adrs/adr-001.md`
51	- Multiple `network-rename`, `assistant-ui-hard-cut`, `workspace-menu-hardcut` plans in `.codex/plans/`
52	- `../analysis/analysis_local_runs.md` lesson LL-1
53

1	# L-012 — TechSpec without Go interface signatures triggers heavy review rework
2	
3	**Class:** Spec authoring
4	**Date discovered:** 2026-04-25 / 2026-04-26 (autonomy techspec vs. release-adjustments comparison)
5	**Evidence sources:** `../analysis/analysis_compozy_tasks.md` §PRD/TechSpec Quality Patterns
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	Two TechSpecs from the same period delivered radically different review trajectories:
10	
11	- **Autonomy techspec** (`.compozy/tasks/autonomous/_techspec.md`, 706 lines): MVP boundary at top, listed Architectural Boundaries, Go interface signatures pasted as code blocks (`ClaimCriteria`, `ClaimedRun`, `TaskClaimer`, `SpawnOpts`, `PermissionNarrower`), data-model fields with rationale, side-table-vs-JSON decisions explicit, lease invariants enumerated as a numbered list. Eighteen tasks executed cleanly with **one** review round.
12	- **Release-adjustments / qa-review** (no `_techspec.md`, just review-only directories): unresolved review queues persisted across multiple PRs. Tasks 07-09 of autonomy that touched contract-laden interfaces had **exactly one** round of fixes because the techspec gave the implementer no contract ambiguity.
13	
14	The differentiator was not length — it was concreteness. Specs that paste signatures, list fields with rationale, and enumerate invariants leave nothing to interpretation. Specs that describe the same mechanics in prose force the implementer to invent shapes that reviewers then reject.
15	
16	## Root cause
17	
18	Prose-only descriptions produce N implementations, where N is the number of agents that read the spec. Reviewers then converge each implementation toward the implicit intent through review rounds — that is the rework. Code blocks (interface signatures, struct fields, SQL DDL, enum values) are unambiguous; reviewers either approve or reject specific tokens, and the spec author resolves the ambiguity once instead of N times.
19	
20	## Rule
21	
22	> A TechSpec is not ready for review until it carries the **six quality markers**:
23	>
24	> 1. MVP boundary statement at top.
25	> 2. Architectural Boundaries section.
26	> 3. Concrete Go interface signatures pasted as code blocks (not prose).
27	> 4. Data-model field rationale (purpose + shape per new column / frontmatter field / config key).
28	> 5. Side-table-vs-JSON decision stated for every new domain entity.
29	> 6. Lease / safety invariants as a numbered list.
30	>
31	> Specs without these markers are pre-rejected — they will need multiple review rounds.
32	
33	## Operationalization
34	
35	`cy-spec-peer-review` invokes Opus with a six-marker checklist embedded in the prompt. `cy-spec-preflight` blocks `cy-create-techspec` from completing until the six markers are present.
36	
37	When a spec is missing a marker, fix the spec — do not start tasks against the gap.
38	
39	## Anti-patterns
40	
41	- "The function will accept the relevant config and return the result." (no signature)
42	- "Add a column for ownership tracking." (no rationale, no name, no type)
43	- "Choose the appropriate storage shape." (no decision)
44	- "Ensure the lease is held safely." (no invariants)
45	- "We'll figure out the interface during implementation." (defers the ambiguity)
46	
47	## Source
48	
49	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/_techspec.md` — the cleanest exemplar
50	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/adrs/adr-003.md` (lease invariants numbered)
51	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/adrs/adr-006.md` (safe spawn caps numbered)
52	- `.compozy/tasks/release-adjustments/` and `.compozy/tasks/qa-review/` — review-only dirs without TechSpecs, persistent unresolved review queues
53	- `../analysis/analysis_compozy_tasks.md` §"Markers of 'good enough to execute'" and §"Markers of trouble"
54	- `docs/_memory/_synthesis.md` skill candidate S-M9 `agh-techspec-quality-gate`
55	
```

1	# L-014: Runtime Vocabulary Must Match Public Contracts
2	
3	**Class:** Architecture / Product vocabulary
4	
5	## Incident
6	
7	The execution isolation feature was implemented and exposed as "environments" even though the product concept was Sandbox. The mismatch appeared across internal packages, config keys, database columns, API fields, CLI flags, hook topics, extension Host API methods, web navigation, settings UI, generated docs, and task artifacts.
8	
9	The feature was also under-documented: the landing page did not explain Sandbox, runtime docs did not have a dedicated Sandbox section, and the web UI hid the feature under Settings instead of giving it primary navigation.
10	
11	## Root cause
12	
13	The first implementation used an overloaded infrastructure term and let it harden into contracts before product vocabulary was settled. Once code, docs, generated references, and QA artifacts diverged, the feature became difficult to discover and easy to misrepresent.
14	
15	## Fix / Rule
16	
17	Public runtime concepts need one canonical noun before they reach contracts. If the noun changes during greenfield alpha, perform one hard cut across every public and internal surface in the same change:
18	
19	- runtime packages, storage columns, config keys, generated contracts, CLI flags, hook names, Host API methods, logs, and tests
20	- web routes, navigation, page copy, mocks, route tests, and generated client types
21	- landing pages, docs navigation, runtime guides, generated CLI docs, and examples
22	- `CLAUDE.md` / `AGENTS.md`, `.compozy/tasks/*` artifacts, glossary, and lessons
23	
24	Do not keep aliases, redirects, dual fields, or fallback parsing for the obsolete product noun. Generic operating-system terminology remains valid when it describes process environment variables or host context.
25	
26	## Evidence
27	
28	- Accepted implementation plan: `.codex/plans/2026-04-28-sandbox-hard-cut.md`
29	- Runtime package hard cut: `internal/sandbox/`
30	- Public API/schema source: `internal/api/spec/spec.go`
31	- Web IA hard cut: `web/src/routes/_app/sandbox.tsx` and `web/src/components/app-sidebar.tsx`
32	- Dedicated docs: `packages/site/content/runtime/core/sandbox/index.mdx`
33	- Landing page surface: `packages/site/components/landing/sandbox-section.tsx`
34

23	// Interaction tracks one directed interaction inside one channel.
24	type Interaction struct {
25		ID        string
26		Channel   string
27		Initiator string
28		Target    string
29		State     InteractionState
30		CreatedAt time.Time
31		UpdatedAt time.Time
32	}
33	
34	// Validate reports whether the interaction carries a usable identity and state.
35	func (i Interaction) Validate() error {
36		if i.ID == "" {
37			return fmt.Errorf("%w: interaction id is required", ErrMissingField)
38		}
39		if i.Channel == "" {
40			return fmt.Errorf("%w: interaction channel is required", ErrMissingField)
41		}
42		if err := ValidateChannel(i.Channel); err != nil {
43			return fmt.Errorf("validate interaction channel: %w", err)
44		}
45		if i.Initiator == "" {
46			return fmt.Errorf("%w: interaction initiator is required", ErrMissingField)
47		}
48		if err := ValidatePeerID(i.Initiator); err != nil {
49			return fmt.Errorf("%w: interaction initiator", err)
50		}
51		if i.Target == "" {
52			return fmt.Errorf("%w: interaction target is required", ErrMissingField)
53		}
54		if err := ValidatePeerID(i.Target); err != nil {
55			return fmt.Errorf("%w: interaction target", err)
56		}
57		if err := i.State.Validate(); err != nil {
58			return fmt.Errorf("validate interaction state: %w", err)
59		}
60		return nil
61	}
62	
63	// IsTerminalState reports whether the state is terminal under the RFC.
64	func IsTerminalState(state InteractionState) bool {
65		switch state {
66		case StateCompleted, StateFailed, StateCanceled:
67			return true
68		default:
69			return false
70		}
71	}
72	
73	// IsParticipant reports whether the peer owns the interaction lifecycle.
74	func (i Interaction) IsParticipant(peerID string) bool {
75		return peerID == i.Initiator || peerID == i.Target

79		switch peerID {
80		case i.Initiator:
81			return i.Target, true
82		case i.Target:
83			return i.Initiator, true
84		default:

86		}

1	# L-005 — Authoritative primitives are exclusive
2	
3	**Class:** Architecture
4	**Date discovered:** 2026-04-25 (autonomy ADR-004); reinforced 2026-04-26 (autonomy task_11)
5	**Evidence sources:** ADR-004 + task_11 memory + global_runs analysis
6	
7	## Context
8	
9	The mechanical scheduler in `internal/scheduler` was tempted to claim runs directly during sweeps and recoveries. That would have collapsed the agent-pull model into a daemon-push model and given two components authority over the same state transition.
10	
11	## Root cause
12	
13	When two components can perform the same authoritative state transition (claim, spawn, migrate, narrow), they will eventually disagree. Either you serialize them (introducing locks, latency, and complexity), or you accept two sources of truth (introducing races and recovery bugs). The clean answer is to pick one authority per transition and force everything else to _observe and notify_ rather than _act_.
14	
15	## Rule
16	
17	> When an authoritative primitive owns a state transition (`task.Service.ClaimNextRun`, `Spawn`, `EnsureMigration`), no peer package may replicate the transition. Wake/observe/sweep are allowed; claim/own is not.
18	
19	## Examples (canonical authorities in AGH)
20	
21	| Transition                    | Authority                            | Allowed peers                                                  |
22	| ----------------------------- | ------------------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------------- |
23	| Claim a `task_run`            | `task.Service.ClaimNextRun`          | `internal/scheduler` may wake idle agents; never claims itself |
24	| Spawn a child session         | Daemon-managed safe-spawn API        | Coordinator submits requests; the daemon decides               |
25	| Apply a schema migration      | `internal/store` migrations registry | `EnsureSchema` is forbidden for column changes                 |
26	| Mutate session terminal state | Session manager                      | Channels, hooks, observability emit events but cannot mutate   |
27	| Approve / publish a task      | Operator + manual API surface        | Coordinators receive enqueues; do not auto-approve             |
28	
29	## Operationalization
30	
31	- **Scheduler can wake and sweep, but cannot claim.** `internal/scheduler` issues `scheduler.wake.count`/`no_match`/`lease_sweep.count`/`error` metrics — never `task.run.claim.success`.
32	- **Hooks can deny/narrow/annotate but cannot bypass safety invariants** (claim tokens, leases, TTL, lineage, spawn caps, permission narrowing).
33	- **Coordination channels are NEVER an ownership/status authority.** Channel `status`/`result` messages cannot mutate ownership/terminal state.
34	
35	## Anti-pattern
36	
37	- Adding a "scheduler.\*" hook taxonomy that lets external code claim runs.
38	- Letting the coordinator bypass `ClaimNextRun` through a "fast-path" for trusted runs.
39	- Allowing the network layer to write terminal state via channel messages.
40	
41	## Source
42	
43	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/adrs/adr-004.md` — Split Semantic vs Mechanical Scheduling
44	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/adrs/adr-009.md` — Hooks First-Class (forbids hook-as-claim)
45	- `.compozy/tasks/autonomous/memory/task_11.md` — scheduler scope-creep avoided
46	- `../analysis/analysis_global_runs.md` lesson L4
47

1	# Standing Directives
2	
3	Ongoing engineering posture, not date-stamped per-task plans. These are perpetually active rules. Surfaced from undated entries in `.codex/plans/` and verified across the synthesis corpus.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## SD-001 — Long-Running Sessions Supervision
8	
9	**Posture.** AGH sessions can run for hours. Supervise activity, don't wait on wall-clock timeouts. Heartbeats, progress events, and idempotent cancel are the supervision primitives.
10	
11	**Required behavior:**
12	
13	- Activity supervisor per prompt/session inspired by Hermes; no wall-clock timeouts as the supervision primitive.
14	- Heartbeats update **metadata only** — never flow through the ACP event channel (backpressure risk). `runtime_progress` is a low-cadence persisted event.
15	- Warnings emit exactly once per session per cause; subsequent inactivity triggers cancel-with-grace before `StopTimeout`.
16	- `CancelPrompt`, session stop, and timeout collapse into ONE idempotent cancellation path.
17	- Inactivity timeout MUST NOT be implemented as a wall-clock timeout. Heartbeat must be positive; `0` disables warning/timeout/progress.
18	- Configuration under `[session.supervision]` with explicit zero-value semantics.
19	
20	**Source:** `.codex/plans/long-running-sessions.md` (undated standing directive). Hermes Track 03 ACP & Session Lifecycle Hardening implements this.
21	
22	**Triggers re-evaluation when:** any change to `internal/session/manager_*.go`, `internal/acp/client.go` lifecycle paths, or supervisor configuration is proposed.
23	
24	---
25	
26	## SD-002 — Remove Legacy Alpha Compatibility Code
27	
28	**Posture.** AGH is greenfield alpha with zero production users. Compat shims, nil-receiver legacy stubs, legacy-meta no-ops, and dual code paths for "old behavior" are forbidden. **Delete the old thing.** This is a stronger, perpetual application of the CLAUDE.md "Greenfield Alpha — Zero Legacy Tolerance" rule.
29	
30	**Required behavior:**
31	
32	- Strip nil-receiver fallbacks, `legacySessionMeta` mappers, no-op compat methods, and "preserve old field name" branches.
33	- Renames are **hard cuts**: code, storage, APIs, CLI, extensions, specs, RFCs, and `.compozy/tasks/*` artifacts in the same change. No aliases, no dual fields.
34	- Distinguish "still useful current logic" (`Reconcile` at composition root) from "legacy support" (delete). Composition-root reconciliation is NOT legacy.
35	- One-pass legacy repair is allowed only in the narrow case where the cost of "delete the old thing" is "every developer rebuilds their local SQLite". Document the boundary in an ADR (e.g., `session-driver-override/adrs/adr-005.md`) and switch to strict semantics immediately after repair.
36	
37	**Source:** `.codex/plans/remove-legacy-alpha.md` (undated standing directive). Multiple ADRs reinforce.
38	
39	**Triggers re-evaluation when:** any PR proposing schema-version branching, `// legacy` markers, dual-naming, or "preserve for compat" logic.
40	
41	---
42	
43	## SD-003 — Conversation in BR-PT, Artifacts in English
44	
45	**Posture.** Pedro types/speaks in Brazilian Portuguese; all persistent artifacts are English.
46	
47	**Required behavior:**
48	
49	- Respond in BR-PT when prompted in BR-PT.
50	- TechSpecs, ADRs, `_idea.md`, `_tasks.md`, code, tests, comments, commit messages, documentation, ledger files, memory files: English.
51	- Verbatim user quotes preserved in evidence/research artifacts may keep BR-PT (because they're evidence).
52	- BR-PT pushback markers ("fraco", "leviano", "ruim", "está totalmente errado", "meia boca", "esquecendo coisas") are escalation signals — slow down and re-clarify.
53	
54	**Source:** `.codex/ledger/` notes; recurring pattern across all sessions.
55	
56	---
57	
58	## SD-004 — Multi-LLM Development Pipeline
59	
60	**Posture.** AGH development uses three LLMs with deliberate role assignment.
61	
62	**Required behavior:**
63	
64	- **Codex (`gpt-5.4` with `reasoning_effort=xhigh`)** authors TechSpecs, major Go code, autonomous-mode kernel work.
65	- **Claude Opus (`xhigh`)** pressure-tests TechSpecs in user-directed cross-LLM review rounds, reviews architecture decisions, writes/reviews React/E2E frontend code.
66	- **`gpt-5.4-mini` with `reasoning_effort=high`** runs as parallel subagents for breadth (codebase mapping, competitor analysis, conversation-log auditing) when explicitly delegated.
67	- Do not substitute models without explicit user approval.
68	- Subagents are read-only — they return analysis to the parent agent, and the parent writes any required files.
69	
70	**Source:** Direct quotes across many sessions; codified in `feedback_multi_llm_pipeline.md` (user memory).
71	
72	---
73	
74	## SD-005 — Real-Scenario QA Before Release
75	
76	**Posture.** `make verify` is necessary but not sufficient. Real-scenario QA against a multi-agent / multi-channel / multi-task workspace catches drift `make verify` misses.
77	
78	**Required behavior:**
79	
80	- Every program ends with a `qa-report` task and a `qa-execution` task. UI-bearing features include browser-based e2e.
81	- Use the `real-scenario-qa` skill for release validation; it delegates to `qa-execution` and `qa-report` for inner mechanics.
82	- Hermetic QA still respects each provider's auth contract: bound-secret and brokered lanes isolate `PROVIDER_HOME`, while `native_cli` providers with `home_policy=operator` keep the operator `HOME` / native login state unless the scenario explicitly validates isolated provider-home behavior.
83	- Concrete bug evidence (autonomy task_18 BUG-001/002/003, Hermes BUG-001..007) shows the QA pass surfaces real production bugs the unit/lint/build coverage cannot catch.
84	
85	**Source:** Codex sessions (most-repeated request); `real-scenario-qa` SKILL.md; autonomy and Hermes QA verification reports.
86	
87	---
88	
89	## SD-006 — Forensic-First Bug Fixes
90	
91	**Posture.** Every bug-fix plan opens with a confirmed reproduction (timestamp, command, observed evidence) BEFORE listing changes. "I think" or "probably" at the top of a fix plan is forbidden.
92	
93	**Required behavior:**
94	
95	- Reproduce the bug with the narrowest real command before editing code.
96	- Record reproduction in the plan: timestamp, exact command, observed output.
97	- Distinguish symptom from root cause in writing.
98	- Fix at root cause; don't patch symptoms.
99	- Add focused regression coverage at the correct layer.
100	- Re-run the narrow reproduction, the impacted scenario, and relevant package tests.
101	
102	**Source:** `.codex/plans/` (consistent forensic frame in `child-workgroup-activation.md`, `session-stop-hang.md`, `dashboard-xterm-visibility.md`, `prompt-stream-stall.md`); also encoded in `real-scenario-qa` and `cy-fix-reviews`.
103	
104	---
105	
106	## SD-007 — Truthful UI > Plausible UI
107	
108	**Posture.** UI must reflect actual backend support. Don't render controls or metrics the runtime doesn't model.
109	
110	**Required behavior:**
111	
112	- When Paper artboards (design references) conflict with daemon truth, **daemon wins**.
113	- Paper governs _composition_; `DESIGN.md` governs _grammar_ (tokens, depth, motion).
114	- No invented controls (per-bridge retry/timeout when runtime doesn't support them).
115	- No invented metrics (no "pending retry" counts when telemetry doesn't expose them).
116	- Observability-only views are allowed (e.g., Network Peers in v1 has no Disconnect/Remove until backend models them).
117	
118	**Source:** Multiple plans in `.codex/plans/` (automation-bridges-paper-redesign, network-paper-pages, bridge-web-e2e).
119	
120	---
121	
122	## SD-008 — Composition Root Discipline
123	
124	**Posture.** Only `daemon/` wires components. Reconciliation logic running at boot belongs to composition root and is NOT "legacy support."
125	
126	**Required behavior:**
127	
128	- New cross-cutting wiring goes in `internal/daemon/`. Never in subordinate packages.
129	- Boot reconciliation (`Reconcile`) is composition-root current logic, not legacy.
130	- Subordinate packages define interfaces and accept implementations via constructors / functional options.
131	- No back-pointers between subordinate packages.
132	- The package import graph flows downward only. `internal/daemon` is the only multi-importer.
133	- `mage Boundaries` is the CI-enforced check; update it in the same commit that introduces a new internal subpackage.
134	
135	**Source:** Root CLAUDE.md Architecture Principles; `_techspec.md` autonomy boundaries; `.codex/plans/observability-spine.md`, `kb-refac-full-sweep.md`, `remove-legacy-alpha.md`.
136	
137	---
138	
139	## SD-009 — Data Exists / Consumer Missing — Build the Consumer
140	
141	**Posture.** When multiple independent investigations converge on the same data structure as "right shape but unconsumed", the gap is integration ergonomics, not architecture.
142	
143	**Required behavior:**
144	
145	- Don't redesign the data when independent slices flag the same data as "right shape, no consumer."
146	- Build the consumer; preserve the data shape.
147	- Surface convergence explicitly in research artifacts ("8 of 10 slices flagged the same six lines").
148	- The autonomy program is the canonical case study: AGH was 80% built before autonomy started; the work was integration, not invention.
149	
150	**Source:** `analysis/analysis_global_runs.md` finding 7; `autonomous/analysis/analysis.md`.
151	
152	---
153	
154	## SD-010 — Detached Execution Lifetime
155	
156	**Posture.** Any work that outlives an HTTP/UDS request — prompts, network channel sends, automation jobs — MUST detach via `context.WithoutCancel(ctx)`. Never tie execution lifetime to request lifetime.
157	
158	**Required behavior:**
159	
160	- Call long-lived work with `context.WithoutCancel(c.Request.Context())` so client disconnect stops streaming, not execution.
161	- Expose explicit cancel endpoints (e.g., `POST /api/sessions/:id/prompt/cancel`).
162	- `context.WithoutCancel` does NOT preserve deadlines — re-attach a deadline if needed.
163	- The four-cause prompt-stream-stall incident (2026-04-20) is the canonical illustration: HTTP request lifetime tied to prompt → tool_call closed stream → web stop using transport abort → metadata repair classifying `m.pending` as crashed. Each was a separate symptom of the same lifetime-coupling root cause.
164	
165	**Source:** `.codex/plans/prompt-stream-stall.md`; `_synthesis.md` lesson L1 (4-analysis evidence).
166	
167	---
168	
169	## SD-011 — Extensible and Agent-Manageable by Design
170	
171	**Posture.** AGH is not only a daemon with UI. It is an extensible runtime that agents must be able to inspect, configure, operate, and repair through structured surfaces. A feature is incomplete if it cannot be extended by AGH's extension surfaces or managed by agents without relying on the web UI.
172	
173	**Required behavior:**
174	
175	- Every PRD, TechSpec, `_tasks.md`, and task body that creates, updates, or removes a feature states the impact on AGH extensibility surfaces: extensions, hooks, skills/capabilities, tools/resources, bundles, registries, bridge SDKs, MCP sidecars, and protocol docs.
176	- Every user-visible or operator-visible capability has an agent-manageability plan: CLI verbs with structured output, HTTP/UDS parity when daemon state crosses the boundary, deterministic error contracts, discoverable status, and documentation for the agent path.
177	- Every CLI command, HTTP endpoint, UDS route, generated contract type, and site reference is added, updated, or deleted in the same change as the feature it manages.
178	- Every `config.toml` addition, update, removal, or no-longer-needed key is handled as a lifecycle change: structs, defaults, merge/overlay behavior, validation, examples, docs, and tests move together.
179	- "No impact" is allowed only with evidence: the artifact names the checked surfaces and explains why no extension, agent-operation, or config change is needed.
180	
181	**Source:** explicit user directive on 2026-04-26; reinforces AGH's product premise (`agent-first`, highly extensible, highly configurable).
182	
183	**Triggers re-evaluation when:** any spec/feature changes runtime behavior, public contracts, CLI verbs, HTTP/UDS routes, config keys, hooks, extension manifests, skill/tool/resource surfaces, bridge SDKs, or agent-operated workflows.
184
